doc_id: A foundation for differentiable logics using dependent type theory
filename: A foundation for differentiable logics using dependent type theory.md
all_wikilinks: On Robustness Metrics for Learning STL Tasks
body_preview:
Many differentiable logics (beyond [[differentiable fuzzy logics]], eg [[signal temporal logic|STL]]-like stuff) have more desirable analytic properties, but are not well understood from an [[algebra|algebraic]] point of view. See eg [[On Robustness Metrics for Learning STL Tasks]]. 

Their [[proof-theoretic semantics]] is also poorly understood.

doc_id: A semantic loss function for deep learning with symbolic knowledge
filename: A semantic loss function for deep learning with symbolic knowledge.md
body_preview:
How should you design a loss function to teach neural networks about background knowledge? This paper uses simple probabilistic logics to achieve this. Computationally this is hard, so they use [[probabilistic circuits]] where the structure is given by a [[knowledge compilation]] algorithm

doc_id: A-nesi_ A scalable approximate method for probabilistic neurosymbolic inference
filename: A-nesi_ A scalable approximate method for probabilistic neurosymbolic inference.md
all_wikilinks: Neural probabilistic logic programming in DeepProbLog
body_preview:
Current methods for neurosymbolic using [[probabilistic logics]] (eg [[Neural probabilistic logic programming in DeepProbLog|DeepProbLog]] rely on exact computation of the probabilities, but this is quite inefficient and often doesn't scale well. So use [[deep generative models]] which estimate the weighted model count used in probabilistic logics. 
![[Pasted image 20251028164309.png]]

body_preview:
> [!abstract]-
> 

How to map vision embeddings to the latent space of an LLM? Use [[convex]] combinations of existing vocabulary embeddings by predicting distributions over tokens. 

Why? Using MLP to transform embeddings can result in OOD embeddings:
![[Pasted image 20251001170552.png]]
They use an LLM output head for this (clever). 
Sampling from that would probably be kinda bad since the tokens are independent. But you could stick a discrete diffusion model on top instead. 

What's nice abou

Basically [[Semantic Probabilistic Layers for Neuro-Symbolic Learning|SPL]] + discrete diffusion. I think they only implement the argmax generation, not constrained sampling. 
They use [[block diffusion]] of length $d$. 
Only works for [[deterministic finite automaton|DFA]]s (ie [[regular expressions|regular language]]s). 

## Method

- A decoder is **valid** if any generation it makes h

body_preview:
> [!abstract]-
> 

(Note, this is before the [[The Platonic Representation Hypothesis]])

A previous work essentially did [[Blip-2_ Bootstrapping language-image pre-training with frozen image encoders and large language models|BLIP-2]] to align the image and text modalities with a linear layer to show the embeddings are aligned. This linear layer was trained using 3M image-caption pairs. 

Is that training step necessary? 

First, they show very high alignment of [[Algorithms for learning kernel

all_wikilinks: On the independence assumption in neurosymbolic learning
body_preview:
We showed in [[On the independence assumption in neurosymbolic learning]] that the common assumption in Neurosymbolic learning that concepts are [[independence|independent]] from each other given the input, can really impact [[uncertainty quantification]]. 

How do we overcome this? One idea is using [[discrete diffusion models|masked diffusion]], a very expressive kind of [[deep generative models]]. This can result in [[Neurosymbolic Diffusion Models]].

Procedural knowledge in pretraining drives reasoning in large language models
A vision check-up for language models
Frozen transformers in language models are effective visual encoder layers
body_preview:
> [!abstract]-
> 

Consider pure [[large language model|LLM]]s (not on vision!).
They develop [[visual prior]]s which can be separated into perception and reasoning priors. The latter is developed by pre-training on reasoning-centric data like code and math, and transfers to visual reasoning. 
The perception prior instead emerges diffusely from the corpora and is sensitive to visual instruction tuning data. 

This reminds me of [[Procedural knowledge in pretraining drives reasoning in large lang

## Definition
A function $P\rightarrow Q$ between two [[partially ordered set]]s (eg: numbers!) is monotone iff for all $a, b\in P$, $a\leq b$ implies $f(a)\leq f(b)$.

- Eg [[variational auto-encoder|VAE]], diffusion, etc.

u^*(\bx, t) = \mathbb{E}_{\bx_0

Your absorbing discrete diffusion secretly models the conditional distributions of clean data
Masked diffusion models are secretly time-agnostic masked models and exploit inaccurate categorical sampling

body_preview:
> [!abstract]-
> 

Framework for controllable generation of masked models, does not require task-specific fine-tuning. 

Very simple idea, just use the timestep 0 parameterisation, sample $K$ times, plug in reward function, renormalise, and sample from reweighted average. 

## Preliminaries
### Masked models
Use $U=\Omega=\{i: \bx_i \neq \bm\}$ for unmasked indices and $M$ for the masked indices.

# A Template for Academic Obsidian Use
I've been using Obsidian for 5 years now (early adopter!) for academic research, and have tweaked and simplified my setup to the core ingredients needed. This demo vault provides all plugins and functionality I rely on, on a daily basis; little more is needed. It's 95% core Obsidian!

body_preview:
This is when in typical NeSy predictor setups, a NeSy predictor properly learned the input-output problem (to 100% accuracy), but did not learn the proper concept extractor. Happens because the problem is not [[identifiable]]

Introduced in [[Not all neuro-symbolic concepts are created equal_ Analysis and mitigation of reasoning shortcuts]], but we now have a larger journal article in [[Symbol grounding in neuro-symbolic AI_ A gentle introduction to reasoning shortcuts]]

body_preview:
> [!definition]
> Axioms: $T: [0, 1]^2 \rightarrow [0, 1]$
> - [[commutative|commutativity]] $T(x, y)=T(y, x)$
> - [[associative]]
> - [[monotone function]]
> - Boundary: $T(x, 1)=x$

Implies: $T(x, 0) = 0$, $T(x, y) \leq \min(x, y)$. 

There are also **triangular subnorms**, for which the Boundary doesn't hold, but $T(x, y) \leq \min(x, y)$ does. 

## Analytical elements
> [!definition]
> **Idempotent element** is $a$ st $T(a, a)=a$. 
> **Nilpotent element** is $a$ st there is some $n$ st $T(a,

all_wikilinks: Mamba_ Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces
body_preview:
Theoretical proof that linear RNNs (ie with linear transitions) are more parallelisable, and can be viewed as log-depth [[arithmetic circuit]]s. This reveals fundamental tradeoffs between nonlinear RNNs.
